# The Owned Channel Advantage: Why Email Still Wins for B2B

## Hook

Every few years, a new channel arrives with promises to kill email. Slack would replace it. LinkedIn InMail would supersede it. Social selling would make it irrelevant. Yet here we are in 2024, and email marketing continues to deliver returns that no other owned channel can match. For senior marketing leaders allocating budgets under pressure, the data is not ambiguous: email is not a legacy tactic. It is the compounding asset your competitors are underinvesting in.

The evidence base for this claim is robust and growing. Research from example.com/study1 tracked 500 B2B companies over multiple years and found that organisations implementing proper segmentation strategies achieved a median 36x return on investment from email. That figure is not a rounding error or a cherry-picked outlier. It reflects what happens when email is treated as a strategic channel rather than a broadcast mechanism. The companies achieving those returns had invested in list quality, segmentation logic, and message relevance. The ones that had not achieved a fraction of those results.

## The Personalisation Shift

The gap between high-performing and average email programmes has widened significantly as personalisation tools have matured. Analysis from example.com/report2, drawing on data from Fortune 500 companies, found that AI-driven personalisation tools increased average open rates by 26 percent. That is not a marginal lift. In a channel where average open rates hover between 20 and 25 percent for B2B, a 26 percent improvement effectively doubles your engaged audience without spending an additional dollar on acquisition.

The implication for marketing leaders is direct: the question is no longer whether personalisation works. It is whether your organisation has the data infrastructure and operational discipline to implement it at the level your competitors will. The mid-market companies that build this capability now will be significantly harder to displace in three years than those waiting for the technology to become more turnkey.

## The Regulatory Reality

Here is where honest analysis requires acknowledging a genuine constraint. Data from example.com/blog3 documented something that many email marketers have experienced but few have quantified: GDPR and related privacy regulations have reduced effective email list sizes by 15 to 30 percent since 2018. That is a structural headwind, not a temporary adjustment.

Consent fatigue is real. Opt-in rates for new list acquisition are declining across markets where privacy regulation has raised consumer awareness. Cross-border data regulations add compliance complexity that smaller teams struggle to navigate efficiently. These are legitimate costs that deserve to be factored into any honest assessment of email's return profile.

The strategic response, however, is not to deprioritise email. It is to treat list quality as a competitive moat. Every compliance investment and every list cleaning exercise creates a cleaner, more engaged audience that outperforms the volume-focused lists your less rigorous competitors are still relying on. Regulatory friction does not hurt every sender equally. It disproportionately hurts those who have not built consent-first acquisition into their growth model.

## Concession

A fair concession is necessary here. The 36x ROI figure from the academic research is not a universal baseline. It represents companies that had invested meaningfully in segmentation infrastructure before the measurement period. For organisations with fragmented CRM data, outdated contact records, or limited marketing operations capacity, the realistic starting point is considerably lower. Similarly, AI personalisation tools deliver stronger results at scale. The Fortune 500 sample in the analyst research reflects companies with data volumes and technical resources that many mid-market organisations do not yet possess.

If your organisation is still running batch-and-blast campaigns to unsegmented lists and measuring success by open rate alone, the path to 36x ROI is not a single investment. It is a multi-year programme requiring data infrastructure, team capability, and strategic commitment. That is worth stating clearly.

## Why It Still Wins

Despite those caveats, the directional case for email as the primary owned channel remains strong. The core advantage is structural: email sits on infrastructure you control. Algorithm changes at LinkedIn, Meta, or Google affect paid and organic reach overnight. Your email list does not disappear because a platform changed its engagement model. The compounding nature of list quality means that each year of disciplined permission-based acquisition and audience management builds an asset that becomes harder to replicate.

For B2B buyers in the 35 to 54 demographic, email remains the preferred channel for receiving business information and engaging with vendor content. That is not a preference that shifts quickly. The buyers your marketing team is trying to reach today grew up in professional environments where email was the primary medium for substantive business communication. That cognitive habit is durable.

## Conclusion

The conclusion for senior marketing leaders is this: email is not a channel you optimise out of your mix. It is the channel you build a durable competitive advantage around. The data from example.com/study1, example.com/report2, and example.com/blog3 consistently points in the same direction. ROI is real and measurable when segmentation and personalisation are applied. The regulatory environment has raised the barrier to entry for lazy practitioners, which is an advantage for organisations willing to do the work properly.

The organisations that will lead in email marketing over the next five years are not waiting for the channel to prove itself again. They are investing now in the data quality, compliance infrastructure, and personalisation capability that will separate high-performing programmes from the rest. The compounding owned channel advantage belongs to those who start building it before it becomes obvious.
